coredata.dc:title: Non-metallic nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots coupled with g-C3N4 achieve efficient photocatalytic performance

coredata.dc:description: Photocatalysis has been prominently featured as a promising technology for solving environmental problems and energy crises. The incorporation of supported non-metallic cocatalysts is paramount in significantly augmenting the efficiency and performance of semiconductor photocatalysis. N-GQD/CN was successfully prepared and showed excellent performance in terms of light-absorbing capacity, carrier separation efficiency and visible-light driving. The findings underscore the exceptional photocatalytic prowess of the 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN composites, particularly in the degradation of RhB, HCl-TC, and BPA under visible light irradiation. The 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN composite could eliminate 91.2 % RhB in 90 min, remove 72.4 % of the HCl-TC in 180 min, and degrade 47.9 % of the BPA in 4 h. All of the activities of the 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN was superior to that of the monomer g-CN. This elevated photocatalytic performance can be predominantly attributed to the N-GQDs acting as co-catalysts. These N-GQDs possess substantial specific surface areas, effectively enhancing light absorption and facilitating synergistic interactions with g-CN nanosheets. Exploring the utility of N-GQD/CN using ionic interference and pH photocatalysis experiments. The present work provides a rational strategy for designing 0D/2D photocatalysts with improved visible light activity for environmental applications.

head.ce:title: Non-metallic nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots coupled with g-C3N4 achieve efficient photocatalytic performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Composite materials comprising nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) modified graphite nitride nanosheets (g-CN) were successfully synthesized via electrostatic adsorption. These N-GQDs not only exhibit uniform dispersion across the g-CN surface but also significantly enhance the number of active sites available. Functioning as co-catalysts, N-GQDs efficiently optimize electron migration pathways, resulting in heightened photocatalytic activity and excellent reproducibility within the composites. Notably, the photocatalytic degradation is attributed to the contributions of ·O2, 1O2, ·OH, and h+. Furthermore, examining the composite performance under varying pH levels and ionic interferences is pivotal for realizing the practicality of environmental photocatalysis applications.

Photocatalysis has been prominently featured as a promising technology for solving environmental problems and energy crises. The incorporation of supported non-metallic cocatalysts is paramount in significantly augmenting the efficiency and performance of semiconductor photocatalysis. N-GQD/CN was successfully prepared and showed excellent performance in terms of light-absorbing capacity, carrier separation efficiency and visible-light driving. The findings underscore the exceptional photocatalytic prowess of the 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN composites, particularly in the degradation of RhB, HCl-TC, and BPA under visible light irradiation. The 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN composite could eliminate 91.2 % RhB in 90 min, remove 72.4 % of the HCl-TC in 180 min, and degrade 47.9 % of the BPA in 4 h. All of the activities of the 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN was superior to that of the monomer g-CN. This elevated photocatalytic performance can be predominantly attributed to the N-GQDs acting as co-catalysts. These N-GQDs possess substantial specific surface areas, effectively enhancing light absorption and facilitating synergistic interactions with g-CN nanosheets. Exploring the utility of N-GQD/CN using ionic interference and pH photocatalysis experiments. The present work provides a rational strategy for designing 0D/2D photocatalysts with improved visible light activity for environmental applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, energy and environmental concerns have become a matter of utmost importance. The adverse climate conditions, energy scarcity, and environmental pollution cannot be overlooked [1,2]. It is imperative to address these issues using sustainable and eco-friendly approaches. Among the most advanced chemical technologies, photocatalysis stands out as a remarkably environmentally friendly clean energy technology that finds widespread applications. Photocatalysis involves the utilization of photocatalysts to absorb light energy, generating photogenerated carriers that are subsequently separated and transferred to the catalyst surface, where a redox reaction takes place. However, many commonly used photocatalysts, such as TiO2 [3], g-C3N4 [4,5], and CdS [6], suffer from poor photocatalytic performance due to the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. This challenge can be effectively tackled by incorporating co-catalysts, which greatly enhance the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. Co-catalysts, acting as electron acceptors, promote charge separation and create more reactive sites. They encompass a range of materials, including noble metals (Pt [7], Ag [8]), non-precious metals (Cu [9], Co [10]), non-metals (CQDs (Carbon quantum dot) [11], GQDs (Graphene quantum dots) [12]), and transition metal compounds (WO3 [13], PtS2 [14]). However, due to their scarcity and high cost, the use of precious metals is limited. Therefore, it is currently advisable to opt for low-cost, high-performance non-metallic materials as co-catalysts.

Among the diverse range of carbon-based materials, graphene stands out as an appealing choice due to its layer-like structure, resembling that of carbon nitride. Variants such as graphene oxide [15], monolayer graphene [16], and GQDs [17,18] have gained significant attention. Notably, GQDs have garnered considerable interest due to their non-toxic and environmentally friendly characteristics, as well as their quantum confinement and edge effects [19,20]. As zero-dimensional materials, GQDs exhibit strong photoelectric properties and quantum size effects. Additionally, GQDs possess a larger specific surface area and adjustable band gap compared to graphene. To further enhance performance and optimize their structure, doping GQDs has emerged as a rapid and effective method. It processes aids in adjusting the band structure and optical properties of the quantum dots. Meanwhile, doping elements are of great interest for their ability to enhance quantum yield [21]. The electronic structure of GQDs can be modulated by atomic doping to drink the energy levels and thus achieve the enhancement of optical-electrical properties [22]. Among the various dopants, nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) exhibit enhanced light absorption capabilities and demonstrate superior activity [23]. The introduction of nitrogen enables better regulation of the energy barrier for electron transport within GQDs, thereby accelerating electron migration rates. N-GQDs also provide more favorable active sites within photocatalytic systems, enhancing adsorption capacity, reducing chemical reaction barriers, and promoting the efficient separation and activation of charges during photocatalysis. Consequently, when N-GQDs are employed as cocatalysts to modify g-C3N4, they exhibit immense potential in compensating for the inherent limitations of g-C3N4. N-GQDs serve as cocatalysts, effectively leveraging their properties to improve the total performance of the photocatalytic system.

In this study, we developed composites by creatively utilizing N-GQDs as co-catalysts in conjunction with g-CN through a simple electrostatic adsorption method. The successful synthesis of the material was confirmed through comprehensive characterization of the morphological structure and optoelectronic properties of the N-GQD/CN composite. The photocatalytic activity of the N-GQD/CN composite was evaluated by purifying three distinct types of pollutants. Encouragingly, the results demonstrated a significant enhancement in the photocatalytic activity of N-GQD/CN compared to g-CN. This improvement can be attributed to the introduction of N-GQDs, which effectively suppress the recombination of photogenerated carriers. To gain further insights into the catalytic process, radical trapping experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) characterization were conducted, enabling the identification of the main active species involved. This work sheds new light on the application of N-GQDs as co-catalysts for modifying g-CN, presenting a promising avenue for future research in the field of photocatalysis.
As shown in Table S1, all reagents were analytically pure and did not require further purification in this study.

Place urea (10 g) in a crucible (50 mL). In a muffle furnace, the temperature was increased to 550 °C for 4 h. White fluffy two-dimensional g-C3N4 was obtained. The monomers are all two-dimensional g-C3N4 referred to as g-CN in this paper [24].

A solution of yellow 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (0.1 g) was initially dispersed in 1 mol/L ammonia for the synthesis of N-GQDs. The resulting suspension was then carefully transferred into a 1-liter autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) and subjected to heating at 180 °C for a duration of 10 h. Following the reaction, the mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature. Subsequently, the product, containing water-soluble GQDs, was subjected to filtration through a 0.22 mm microporous membrane to eliminate any insoluble carbon byproducts. To further purify the sample, it was dialyzed within a dialysis bag with a molecular weight retention limit of 3,500 Da for a period of 2 days. This process effectively removed sodium salts and smaller, unreacted molecules. The resulting purified samples were collected in 50 mL centrifuge tubes and reserved for subsequent experiments. It's worth noting that the concentration of N-GQDs utilized in this study was 0.6 mg/mL. [25,26].

A certain amount of N-GQD sonication is uniformly dispersed into 20 mL of ethanol for 30 min. Then the g-CN (50 mg) was added to the above mixture with ultrasound for 10 min and stirring for 12 h at room temperature. The product was dried at 80 °C named N-GQD/CN. According to the content of N-GQDs, the obtained sample is named X wt.% N-GQD/CN (X = 0.01, 0.02, 0.06, 0.24, 0.36, 0.48).

The evaluation encompassed several analytical techniques. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was employed using a Nicolet Nexus 470 instrument, while X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis utilized a Shimadzu XRD-6000 instrument to examine the structural characteristics of the samples. For a comprehensive assessment of the chemical composition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted with a VG MultiLab 2000 instrument. To investigate visible light absorption properties, UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) was performed over the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The study of steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra was facilitated by QuantaMaster and TimeMaster fluorescence spectrophotometers. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photocurrent measurements were carried out employing a CHI 660B instrument in a conventional three-electrode system. Furthermore, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was conducted using a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer, aimed at elucidating the presence of active species within the system. This multifaceted approach provided a comprehensive understanding of the synthesized materials and their photocatalytic performance.

The photocatalytic performance of the samples containing N-GQD/CN was evaluated using three different contaminants: 10 mg/L Rhodamine B (RhB), 10 mg/L Bisphenol A (BPA), and 20 mg/L Tetracycline hydrochloride (HCl-TC). Typically, 15 mg of the photocatalyst was dispersed in a 50 mL solution of RhB, while 25 mg of N-GQD/CN was added to 50 mL solutions of BPA and HCl-TC, respectively. The photocatalytic reactions were equipped with a 250 W xenon arc lamp as the light source (with a filter that allowed wavelengths greater than 400 nm). The suspensions were stirred in the dark for 30 min to establish an equilibrium between adsorption and desorption. The collected samples were centrifuged, and the absorbance degradation of the organic compounds (RhB and HCl-TC) was measured at 554 nm and 356 nm, respectively, using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (T10CS, Puxi Beijing). The concentration of BPA was determined using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with a mobile phase consisting of 75 % methanol and 25 % water by volume. The detection wavelength was set at 230 nm, and the chromatographic analysis was performed on a C18 column (Agilent 1260 Infinity, 4.6 mm × 250 mm, 5 μm diameter). The elution time for BPA was 5 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structural characterization of N-GQD/CN
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic route which is based on N-GQDs as cocatalysts modified g-CN materials, is shown in Scheme 1 . N-GQD/CN was prepared by simple electrostatic attraction. Fig. 1 a illustrates the phase structures of the prepared photocatalysts. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns exhibit strong characteristic peaks at approximately 27.3° and weak characteristic peaks around 13.1°, which can be attributed to the (100) and (002) crystal planes of g-CN, respectively. The 13.1° peak corresponds to the repeating triazine ring plane, while the 27.3° peak represents the graphite accumulation specific to graphitic carbon nitride [27,28]. These findings confirm the typical crystal structure of g-CN. Notably, the composite sample pattern does not exhibit significant characteristic peaks of N-GQDs, indicating that the N-GQDs are present in low quantities and highly dispersed within the composite. Additionally, the characteristic peaks in the composite show minimal changes compared to g-CN, demonstrating that the recombination is not affecting the basic phase structure of g-CN [29–31].

The chemical bonds and functional groups of the samples were characterized using FTIR spectra, as depicted in Fig. 1b. All the prepared samples exhibit typical carbon nitride structure features. The stretching mode of the amino group (N–H) is represented by characteristic peaks observed at 3000–3400 cm−1 [32,33]. Similarly, a characteristic peak is observed at 1200–1700 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching mode of the aromatic CN heterocycle. The breathing pattern at 810 cm−1 primarily represents the tri-s-triazine ring [34,35]. These observations indicate that the N-GQD/CN composites maintain a well-preserved g-CN skeleton structure. Notably, the transmittance of the N–H correlation band gradually increases in the N-GQD/CN sample compared to monomers g-CN, and the characteristic peaks become wider with higher N-GQDs content. This suggests that the composite possesses an increased number of N–H groups on the two-dimensional carbon nitride framework.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) was employed to examine the morphology of the samples. The TEM images in Fig. 2 a and b illustrate the pleated sheet morphology of g-CN, resembling graphene, and reveal its thin and two-dimensional structure. This characteristic makes it conducive for loading cocatalysts onto the surface of g-CN. Fig. 2c displays the uniform dispersion of N-GQDs, with the inset providing a magnified view of N-GQDs exhibiting well-defined crystalline morphology. The lattice spacing value of 0.22 nm coincides with the (100) crystal plane of graphene. Moreover, Fig. 2d depicts the composite N-GQD/CN sample, clearly demonstrating the strong bond formed between N-GQDs and g-CN [36].

The pore size distribution and specific surface area of materials were analyzed using BET and BJH measurements, as depicted in Fig. S1 . The specific surface area and pore size distribution of g-CN and N-GQDs modified g-CN materials were analyzed using BET and BJH measurements, as depicted in Fig. S1 . All samples exhibited a type IV isotherm with the H3 hysteresis loop appearing at a high relative pressure range (0.80–1.00P/P0) [37,38]. The specific surface area of pristine g-CN was determined to be 63.8 m2g−1. Upon the introduction of 0.02 wt.% N-GQDs, a slight increase in the specific surface area (64.3 m2g−1) was observed. The results of these studies indicate that the higher specific surface area of N-GQD/CN is favorable for improving the contact between the catalyst and the pollutant. However, the change in specific surface area was not significant, indicating that the specific surface area was not the main factor affecting the photocatalytic activity [39].

The chemical properties and states of the materials were checked using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. S2 , the survey XPS spectrum demonstrates that both g-CN and 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN mainly consist of C 1s, N 1s, and trace O 1s peaks. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 3 a) revealed three peaks for monomeric g-CN at binding energies of 284.8, 288.5, and 293.3 eV, respectively. The peak at 284.8 eV corresponds to C–C bonds [40–42]. The prominent peak at 288.5 eV is attributed to sp2 hybridized carbon (N=C-N) [34], while the weak peak at 293.3 eV arises from carbon in the aromatic ring attached to the terminal uncondensed NH2 species. Notably, the C 1 s binding energy for the 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN samples exhibited a chemical shift in comparison to g-CN, indicating the interaction between the components. The N 1s spectra of both samples (Fig. 3b) displayed four peaks at binding energies of 398.7, 399.5, 400.9, and 404.2 eV, corresponding to triazine ring sp2 hybridization (C=N-C), N-(C)3, C-N–H, and π excitations, respectively [43,44]. Compared to g-CN, the binding energies in the complex are 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN samples shifted higher, consistent with the FTIR characterization results. These results further confirm the synthesis of 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN samples, indicating that N-GQDs have been successfully introduced into g-CN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and energy band structures
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) of the samples are presented in Fig. 4 a. The absorption edge of g-CN is observed at 430 nm. It is evident from the spectra that the N-GQD in the composite has little effect on the absorption edge, but there is a gradual increase in the light absorption intensity. The observation correlates with the deepening color of the samples as the N-GQDs content increases. The bandgap of g-CN was derived as 2.64 eV (Fig. 4b) [45]. Additionally, the VB values of both g-CN and 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN samples were determined as 1.56 eV based on the VB-XPS spectra (Fig. 4c) [46–48]. The CB value of g-CN, calculated using the equation ECB = EVB-Eg, is −1.08 eV. These results indicate that N-GQDs have a minor effect on the valence band and the bandgap of the material. The positive slope of g-CN was tested as an n-type semiconductor by Mott-Schottky ( Fig. S3 ). It has a flat band potential (Efb) of 1.01 eV (vs. NHE). The calculated Efb = 1.01 eV for g-CN. The ECB of the n-type semiconductor is 0.1–0.3 eV higher than the Efb. The above M−S results do not differ much from the above energy band structure speculation [49]. At the same time, the EVB of g-CN was again verified using ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) in Fig. S4 . The values obtained did not differ much from those of VB-XPS [50].

The behavior of N-GQDs modified g-CN photocatalysts in degrading organic dyes, antibiotics, and phenolic pollutants was investigated, as shown in Fig. 5 . Monomeric g-CN exhibited poor activity for all three types of pollutants. In contrast, the composites modified with N-GQDs demonstrated excellent performance. The photocatalytic activity of the composites exhibited a consistent increase as the weight ratio of N-GQDs was decreased from 0.48 % to 0.02 %. However, the photocatalytic activity tended to decrease when the sample weight ratio reached 0.01 %. (Fig. 5a). Among the composites, 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN showed the highest activity. Under visible light irradiation, 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN degraded 91.2 % of RhB within 90 min, with a degradation rate 1.47 times more than that of pristine g-CN. The degradation rate constants (k) for the RhB degradation by N-GQD/CN were determined (Fig. 5b), and it was evident that 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN exhibited the highest rate [51]. The reaction rate constants (k) for 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN and g-CN were calculated as 0.0303 and 0.00725 min−1, respectively. Clearly, the composites demonstrated superior degradation properties under visible light when the N-GQDs loading reached 0.02 wt%. Additionally, 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN degraded 72.4 % of HCl-TC within 180 min (Fig. 5c) and 47.9 % of BPA during 4 h (Fig. 5d). The composites were found to be 1.20 and 1.28 times more efficient in degrading HCl-TC and BPA, respectively, compared to monomeric g-CN within the specified time. To compare the photocatalytic activity of N-GQD/CN, the photocatalytic degradation performance of RhB, TC-HCl and BPA by different photocatalysts in recent years was listed (Tables S2-S4). Enumeration of photocatalytic degradation properties of other methods of modifying g-CN (Tables S5). The photocatalytic stability and reusability of 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN samples with RhB as the target contaminant were assessed (Fig. 5e). After 10 consecutive cycles of photocatalytic experiments, the activity of the photocatalyst did not significantly decrease, demonstrating the superb stability of N-GQD/CN. XRD tests performed on the recycled samples (Fig. 5f) revealed that the crystal structure of the cycled N-GQD/CN samples remained unchanged, indicating the stable composites after photocatalytic degradation.

To assess the impact of the photocatalyst on the degradation ability of organic dyes in real environmental conditions and facilitate the practical application of photocatalysts, an ionic interference photocatalyst test was conducted using a RhB solution. Different ions at a consistency of 1.0 mmol/L were injected into the photocatalytic system to simulate an aqueous environment. [52]. The influence of cations on the degradation activity of the photocatalysts was investigated (Fig. 6 a) by adding salts such as NaCl, KCl, NH4Cl, CaCl2, and MgCl2. The activity of the samples during the interference of anions is presented in Fig. 6b. The sodium salts used were NaCl, Na2SO4, NaBr, Na2CO3, and NaHCO3. The findings showed that the photocatalytic activity of 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN was not significantly inhibited by the introduction of other ions. The pH of the resolution was adjusted using HNO3 and NaOH. It was observed that under acidic conditions, RhB was more easily degraded, as depicted in Fig. 6c. It can be attributed to the alkaline nature of RhB, which facilitates effective electrostatic adsorption and hydrogen bond formation with the photocatalyst under acidic conditions, thereby promoting the degradation process. Conversely, a higher pH of the solution causes repulsion between RhB and the photocatalyst, leading to reduced activity [52].

To gain further insights into the detailed degradation process of the photocatalyst, this study employed liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (LC-MS) to analyze the possible intermediates, which can provide valuable information for inferring the reaction pathway of RhB. The MS characteristic signal of the product is depicted in Fig. S5 . During the photocatalytic degradation, the pristine RhB (m/z = 443) was observed to be formed from N, N-diethyl rhodamine (m/z = 387) through the loss of two ethyl groups (Fig. 7 ). Subsequently, the gradual removal of ethyl groups led to the formation of two products: N-ethyl-rhodamine 110 (m/z = 359) and RHO-damine 110 (m/z = 331), respectively. In one of the pathways, RHO-damine 110 underwent further degradation by losing a carboxyl group, resulting in the formation of a new product (m/z = 301). The ring-opening reaction proceeded, followed by redox processes, ultimately leading to the formation of CO2 and H2O. Alternatively, RHO-damine 110 (m/z = 331) underwent ring-opening and deamidation reactions, resulting in the formation of glutaric and adipic acids. Finally, complete mineralization occurred, yielding CO2 and H2O [53–55].

Upon subjecting HCl-TC to visible light irradiation, the catalysts undergo the degradation process, leading to the formation of intermediate products and suggesting potential reaction pathways. The photocatalytic degradation process is illustrated in Fig. S6 , where TC molecules are progressively transformed into intermediates with smaller relative molecular weights. Various intermediates were identified at m/z = 466, 405, 342, 314, 270, 262, 178, 104, and so on [56]. A possible reaction pathway for the degradation of TC by 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN is presented in Fig. 8 . Along this pathway, the intermediates undergo successive transformations ultimately resulting in the mineralization of TC, yielding CO2, H2O, and NO3 –.

In investigating the photoelectric properties of the samples, PL (photoluminescence) spectroscopy was employed (Fig. 9 a). A comparison between g-CN and 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN reveals significantly weaker luminescence intensity around 440 nm in the latter, indicating that the introduction of N-GQDs enhances the separation of photoelectrons from holes. Previous reports have established that GQDs exhibit favorable photoluminescence properties, leading to the absorption of more intense light, thereby inducing a higher generation of photogenerated carriers and enhancing photocatalytic activity [57,58]. To further analyze the photoelectric properties, photocurrent response tests and EIS (electrochemical impedance spectroscopy) were conducted using a three-electrode system under vis-light irradiation (Fig. 9b and c). The results demonstrate that the photocurrent intensity of 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN is significantly higher than that of g-CN, and the photocurrent does not decay after six cycles. Additionally, in the EIS Nyquist plot, the 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN composite exhibits a smaller arc radius compared to g-CN [59–61]. The equivalent circuit of the Nyquist diagram was also fitted and the resistance values were derived. The resistance values of g-CN and N-GQD/CN are 442 and 133 Ω, respectively [62]. These findings align with the PL data, indicating improved separation efficiency of carriers in the 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN composites and their stable optoelectronic properties. Overall, these results highlight the remarkable advantages and capabilities of 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN in the field of photocatalysis.

The involvement of active sports in the photocatalytic process was analyzed using radical-induced trapping experiments and ESR (electron spin resonance) tests, providing insights into the reaction mechanism of the system. In Fig. 10 a-c, distinct characteristic signal responses of DMPO-·O2 –, DMPO-·OH, and DMPO-·1O2 can be observed in visible light exposure. It indicates the presence of all three active species in the system under light conditions. Importantly, the characteristic signals of these active species are stronger in the composites compared to monomeric g-CN, highlighting that 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN can generate more ·O2 –, ·OH, and 1O2 radicals. It suggests that these active species, along with holes, contribute to the catalytic process. The presence of N-GQDs likely promotes redox reactions, resulting in an increased generation of active species [50]. To further confirm this conjecture, photocatalytic radical capture degradation experiments were performed on composites using RhB (Fig. 10d). In the absence of TEOA, L-Trp, PBQ and IPA, the degradation effect of RhB was inhibited. Under the influence of L-Trp, only 10.1 % of RhB could be degraded in a limited time. TEOA and PBQ resulted in the degradation of RhB by 40.1 % and 43.4 % respectively, within a certain period. Compared to other substances, IPA had little effect on the degradation rate but still achieved 92 % degradation of organic pollutants within 120 min. Therefore, it can be summarized that ·O2 –, ·OH, ·1O2 and holes all contribute to the photocatalytic process in the N-GQD/CN system, with ·O2 –, ·1O2, and h+ being the most significant [63,64]. The findings of the free radical trapping induction experiments are in agreement with those of the ESR tests.

Based on the previous DSR data, the guide band and valence band values of g-CN were calculated to be −1.08 and 1.56 eV. These values indicate the proper positions of the g-CN valence and conduction bands. Under the irradiation of sunlight, g-CN generates photogenerated electrons and holes. The photogenerated electrons jump to CB and then to N-GQD, while the photogenerated holes are retained on VB, completing the separation of photogenerated carriers in the N-GQD/CN system. The conduction band of g-CN (-1.08 eV vs NHE) is more negative than Eo (O2/·O2 –) (-0.046 eV vs NHE) and Eo (O2/1O2) (0.34 eV vs NHE), indicating that O2 is reduced to produce ·O2 – and 1O2. The ·O2 – species can directly react with H+ to form ·OH. On the other hand, pure g-CN alone cannot oxidize H2O or OH– to generate ·OH due to the inability of its valence band to drive the oxidation of OH– and generate ·OH (Eo (·OH/OH–) = 2.38 eV). However, vacancies on the valence band of g-CN can act as contaminants and undergo transformation into products (Fig. 11 ). In summary, the photocatalytic degradation process generates ·O2 –, 1O2, and ·OH, which further promotes the degradation of organic matter [65,66]. Therefore, ·OH, ·O2 –, and 1O2 are determined as active species that play a decisive role in N-GQD/CN. N-GQD as an active site can effectively align carrier recombination and thus improve photocatalytic performance. The above findings are in accordance with the outcome of ESR spin-trapping tests and free radical trapping experiments.

The photocatalytic activity of N-GQD/CN prepared in this study was significantly enhanced. The results reveal that 0.02 wt.% N-GQD/CN the degradation rate k = 0.0303 min−1 for RhB. It still showed extraordinary photocatalytic effects in HCl-TC and BPA. The N-GQDs as co-catalysts g-CN enhance light absorption and provide more active spots for photocatalytic reactions. N-GQD/CN effectively promotes photogenerated carrier separation for highly performing photocatalysis. Additionally, N-GQD/CN demonstrated excellent stability and reusability. In this system, ·O2 and 1O2 are the major active sports involved in the reaction process, while ·OH and h+ also play a significant role. Therefore, the preparation of composites using non-metallic catalysts allows for wider light absorption and efficient separations of electrons and holes. The ideas presented in this study are conducive to the synthesis of 0D/2D photocatalysts and efficient photocatalytic degradation of pollutants, which have great potential for application in the field of environmental water purification.
